problem: Telegram context loss: every turn save/restored agent.messages via conversation_switch; after /clear permanently zero. Quote-reply context silently dropped. Blank LLM response sent as "(empty)". Unclosed HTML tags caused 400 errors.
countermeasure: T-244: replaced conversation_switch with _conv_cache dict on TelegramTools; first contact loads SQLite, subsequent turns use memory. /clear sets cache[conv_id]=[]. T-245: injected [Replying to: "..."] directly into joined bubble text before dispatch. T-246: _send_plain_chunks uses real retry message; _reply_chunks skips blank formatted text; _dispatch_bubble sends fallback on empty reply. T-247: _format_for_telegram closes unclosed tags; _reply_chunks strips HTML before plain-text fallback on 400.

problem: Pi was single-user; no profile concept, no per-user namespace isolation, no separate DBs.
countermeasure: agent/profile.py: Profile dataclass + ProfileRegistry backed by profiles table in pi.db. validate_profile_name() enforces ^[a-z0-9_]{2,24}$ + reserved-name blocklist. profile_to_memory() mirrors _get_memory_for_config — db_path=data/pi_<name>.db, namespace=profile_<name> => _NoopSupabase. Ash implicit default. display_name/nickname fields. 33 tests.
lessons: Moving optional fields after required fields in dataclass prevents TypeError in existing call sites

problem: Telegram gated only on single chat_id; no login, password, per-user identity, or profile management.
countermeasure: agent/profile.py: hash_password pbkdf2_hmac sha256 260k iterations, verify_password, device_bindings table (permanent sticky login). tools/tools_telegram.py: /login resolves profile, hashes pw, creates binding, deletes login msg. /logout, /whoami, /profile create|list|revoke ash-only. Rate-limited lockout after 5 fails. 17 tests.
lessons: pbkdf2_hmac is stdlib sufficient; login message delete is best-effort

problem: Agent had one memory, one consciousness, one namespace for all turns — no per-profile switching.
countermeasure: agent/profile.py: profile_switch(agent, profile) contextmanager saves/restores agent.current_profile, memory, consciousness in finally block. tools/tools_telegram.py: _process_as_telegram_peer wraps guests with profile_switch; nullcontext for Ash. Outermost context so memory resolves before conversation loads. 8 tests.
lessons: profile_switch must be outermost — profile outer, conversation inner

problem: execute_tool had no per-caller capability check; god mode enterable by anyone.
countermeasure: agent/profile.py: GUEST_DENIED_TOOLS (file writes) and GUEST_APPROVAL_TOOLS (execution). agent/tools.py: execute_tool checks is_guest — denied=structured denial; approval=request_execution_approval pending result. God gate refuses unless profile==ash. prompts/consciousness_guest.txt: neutral persona. 7 tests.
lessons: Enforce in dispatch layer not prompt. Fail-closed: unclassified fs-mutating tools treated as denied.

problem: No approval mechanism; guest execution tools had no path to run with Ash oversight.
countermeasure: agent/tools.py: ApprovalStore backed by approvals table in pi.db (token, profile_name, tool, args_json, status, expires_at). request_execution_approval creates record + notifies Ash via Telegram. /approve|/deny|/approvals handlers in tools_telegram.py ash-only. On approve: re-dispatches in guest namespace via profile_switch. Single-use tokens 30min TTL. 6 tests.
lessons: Control-plane (approvals) in Ash pi.db never guest DB. Single-use prevents replay.

problem: Guest tickets landed in tickets/open/ (Ash queue); guest turns logged to shared logs/turns.jsonl.
countermeasure: tools/tools_project.py: create_ticket writes to tickets/profiles/<name>/ for guests; tags with profile field. scripts/sprint.py: extended god-refusal guard to skip tickets/profiles/*. agent/turn_log.py: append_turn routes to logs/profiles/<name>/turns.jsonl for guests; adds profile field. 8 tests.
lessons: Reuse sprint.py path-refusal pattern rather than new filter

problem: Gemini Google-Search grounding untapped; web_search returned raw snippets with no synthesis.
countermeasure: core/providers/gemini.py: grounded_generate() attaches google_search tool, extracts grounding_metadata citations. tools/tools_research.py: grounded_search returns {answer, citations, provider}. Falls back to web_search on Gemini error. Added to normie allowlist. 5 tests.
lessons: web_search fallback mandatory so Gemini outage degrades gracefully

problem: No multi-source synthesis or cross-validation; single-pass web_search had no agreement/conflict signal.
countermeasure: tools/tools_research.py: deep_research runs web_search + grounded_search + awareness for news queries. Dedupes by domain+title; marks corroborated (>=2 domains) or conflicting. Groq synthesizes {summary, key_points, sources, agreement_notes, confidence}. Normie + root. 6 tests.
lessons: Name confidence honestly so Pi never overclaims

problem: Raw LLM text returned untouched; no formatting standard, citation output, or output contract.
countermeasure: prompts/consciousness.txt + consciousness_normie.txt: OUTPUT CONTRACT block (answer-first, scannable, fact vs interpretation, Sources when tools used). Post-step in pi_agent.py appends Sources idempotently when citations exist and model omitted them. No T-192 tool-drift. 5 tests.
lessons: Prompt contract is cheap/high-impact; post-step must be idempotent

problem: Telegram photo handler crashed with IndexError when message.photo was an empty list.
countermeasure: tools/tools_telegram.py handle_photo: added guard `if not message.photo` before accessing message.photo[-1]; replies with friendly resend prompt.
lessons: Defensive indexing on Telegram message.photo — Telegram can send content_types=photo with empty photo array

problem: telegram_react not in normie allowlist → model outputs [telegram_react]{...} as raw text. image_gen blocked in normie. After image_gen in root, model outputs markdown image syntax Telegram can't render.
countermeasure: agent/modes.py: added telegram_react, telegram_send, image_gen to normie tool_allowlist. pi_agent.py _execute_tool: auto-calls send_file when image_gen succeeds and _current_chat_id is set — image delivered immediately without model needing to chain telegram_send.
lessons: Model hallucinating tool calls as text = tool missing from allowlist. Auto-deliver side-effect artifacts at dispatch layer — don't trust model to chain follow-up calls.

problem: /approve and /deny crashed with Telegram API 400 'Unsupported start tag token'. Bot defaults to parse_mode=HTML; usage hint contained literal <token> which Telegram rejected. Byte offsets confirmed: 16 for /approve, 13 for /deny.
countermeasure: tools/tools_telegram.py handle_approvals: added _pm={'parse_mode': None} and applied to all send_message calls in the handler. No HTML needed in any approval response; also guards against result_text or exception strings with accidental HTML chars.
lessons: Global bot parse_mode=HTML means ALL send_message calls parse as HTML unless overridden. Plain-text replies must pass parse_mode=None explicitly.

problem: Bare 'except:' in research_mode.py:94 swallowed KeyboardInterrupt/SystemExit; no repo-wide guard against the pattern recurring.
countermeasure: core/research_mode.py:94 -> except Exception:. scripts/verify.py gained check_bare_except() (ast walk for ExceptHandler with type=None), wired into main() as a hard gate. testing/test_no_bare_except.py.
lessons: Fixing a lint finding by hand invites recurrence; fold it into the gate that found it.

problem: PI.md section 3 (sprint queue) was 8 weeks stale (week of 2026-05-05), so sprint_readiness passive skill FAILed on cadence staleness.
countermeasure: Ran python scripts/plan_sprint.py --auto with the hackathon-prep goal; PI.md section 3 regenerated via the sanctioned tool path with the live P0-first ticket queue.
lessons: plan_sprint.py --auto works non-interactively and produces a better-ordered queue than a hand edit would.

problem: analysis/candidate_tickets.jsonl had unreviewed candidates re-surfacing every miner run (dedup is against ticket titles, not prior candidate rows). One real bug found: ticket_candidate_miner.py's code-marker regex matched 'HACK' inside '## Hacker News' (missing word boundary).
countermeasure: Triaged all candidates: 3 already-stale (tests that now pass), 1 checkpoint-noise (T-148 already closed), 1 false-positive regex bug fixed (added \\b after the marker alternation in scripts/passive/ticket_candidate_miner.py), remainder covered by sibling tickets (sprint_readiness/session_exit cascade).
lessons: A miner that re-surfaces the same finding forever isn't broken — it's telling you the finding was never actually resolved or deliberately dismissed.

problem: passive_daily_digest, session_exit_protocol, and sprint_readiness all reported FAIL; unclear if independent bugs or cascade.
countermeasure: Diagnosed: session_exit_protocol's check_no_fail_reports scans ALL reports/*.md including sibling skills' own (sometimes self-referential) FAIL state; passive_daily_digest aggregates the same. sprint_readiness's git-clean check correctly reports FAIL while the branch is intentionally mid-batch (232+ uncommitted files) pending T-272's Ash-gated landing. Root fixes landed via T-251/T-252/T-275; the git-clean FAIL is accurate and will clear once T-272 lands, not a bug.
lessons: A passive skill that reads other skills' reports can look like 3 independent bugs when it's one cascading state — check the read graph before chasing each FAIL separately.

problem: 26 swallowed-exception sites in tools_telegram.py; audit needed to find genuinely silent, high-impact ones (not the ~15 already correctly instrumented or cosmetic ones like typing-indicator sends).
countermeasure: Instrumented 5 genuinely silent, high-impact sites with track_silent: guest approval/denial Telegram notifications (HITL-critical), _store_media_to_memory (silent data loss of photo/video analysis), /login and /profile create password-message cleanup (security-adjacent). Left ~10 cosmetic sites (typing indicators, polling-stop) untouched — instrumenting them would be noise.
lessons: A static except-count overstates the real gap; most sites in this file were already properly handled. Read each site before deciding it needs instrumentation.

problem: generate_video's chain-exhaustion path silently dropped the Replicate failure reason once HuggingFace also failed — only the last backend's error survived. analyze_document_with_vision's PDF vision-analysis block failed with a bare pass, silently returning success with an empty vision_analysis.
countermeasure: tools/tools_video_gen.py generate_video: collects every attempted backend's error into one combined message ('tried Replicate (reason); HuggingFace (reason)'). tools/tools_media.py analyze_document_with_vision: bare pass -> track_silent('media.analyze_document_with_vision_pdf', e).
lessons: Chain-exhaustion errors must name every backend tried, not just the last one, or the user can't tell what actually happened.

problem: No inbound Gmail triage; unread mail sat unnoticed unless Ash checked manually. No cross-system HITL flow existed end-to-end.
countermeasure: agent/watchers.py: new 'email' watcher type (_check_email) polling gmail_search('is:unread newer_than:1d'), diffing seen ids in existing in-memory watcher state. tools/tools_telegram.py: new send_buttons() + _EMAIL_TRIAGE_PREFIX callback routing so email alerts carry 'Draft reply/Add to calendar/Ignore' buttons that route to explicit gmail_read+gmail_send / calendar_create instructions via the existing T-220 callback handler.
lessons: Telegram callback_data is capped at 64 bytes — encode a compact action:id reference, not the full context, and reconstruct the instruction server-side.

problem: logs/turns.jsonl grows unbounded (every turn, every mode, forever) with no rotation.
countermeasure: agent/turn_log.py: rotate_turns_log() gzips to logs/archive/turns_jsonl-<ts>.jsonl.gz (the exact pattern recent_turns() already reads) once past 50MB, then truncates (never deletes) the live file. Wired as a nightly 03:30 job in tools/tools_scheduler.py plus a standalone scripts/passive/turns_log_rotate.py.
lessons: The reader (recent_turns) was already built to expect gzipped archives in logs/archive/ — the writer was the missing half. Check both sides of a read/write pair before assuming a feature is unbuilt.

problem: No CI; regressions only caught if verify.py was run locally before a push.
countermeasure: .github/workflows/verify.yml: on push/PR, checkout + setup-python 3.13 + pip install -r requirements.txt + python scripts/verify.py --quiet. windows-latest runner (mirrors the dev box, avoids Linux wheel-build pain for opencv/deepface/tf-keras/audio libs).
lessons: Match the CI runner OS to the dev OS when the dependency list is heavy/native — cross-platform wheel issues are a bigger risk than losing Linux-runner speed.

problem: The T-244..T-248 Telegram bug cluster all shipped to production because no test drove the real registered handler closures — existing tests replicated handler logic inline instead.
countermeasure: testing/test_telegram_handlers.py: a fake bot object captures handlers registered via _register_handlers() (real code, not reimplemented logic), then drives handle_text and handle_callback directly. docs/TELEGRAM_SMOKE.md: 5-step manual checklist for what still needs a live Telegram client.
lessons: A fake bot with plain decorator methods (message_handler/callback_query_handler capturing into a list) is enough to drive real handler closures without any network — cheap infrastructure that was simply never built.

problem: Root mode had no way to invoke the 3-agent (Claude/Gemini/Groq) debate mid-conversation; run_research_mode() also had a blocking input() that would hang forever if called outside the interactive REPL.
countermeasure: core/research_mode.py: run_research_mode() gained interactive=True default, guarding the Enter prompt. tools/tools_research.py: new deep_debate ToolSpec (root mode, tool_allowlist=None admits it automatically) calling run_research_mode(interactive=False).
lessons: Any REPL-oriented function being reused as a tool handler needs an audit for blocking input() calls first — they hang a tool-call turn with no error, just silence.

problem: Only scripts/sprint.py escalated failures to Telegram; general runtime P1s (turn crashes, provider-chain exhaustion, session-exit failures) sat invisible in silent_failures.db until someone thought to look.
countermeasure: scripts/passive/silent_failure_watcher.py: send_p1_alerts() matches known P1 categories (agent.process_input exact; *.all_exhausted, *.session_exit_error suffix) against the 24h count, sends one throttled Telegram alert per category per day via a small JSON dedup-state file, reusing tools_telegram.send_message.
lessons: Throttle-by-day-per-category before wiring any new alert path — an alert storm during a real outage is worse than the silence it replaces.

problem: memory_pollution_detector.py scanned vault/.god/ (private god-mode layer) and surfaced its filename (vault/.god/missions.md) in a non-god passive report — a privacy leak. Separately, 168 vault notes lack YAML frontmatter, but nearly all are auto-generated snapshots (sprints/sessions/per-ticket) never designed to carry it.
countermeasure: scripts/passive/memory_pollution_detector.py check_vault_notes: excluded any path containing '.god' from the vault rglob. Frontmatter WARN deliberately left as-is (not mass-edited) — retrofitting 168 auto-generated files for a cosmetic Obsidian convention with no functional dependency is busywork, not hygiene.
lessons: God-mode privacy applies to passive-skill scan scope too, not just publish guards — a read-only report can still leak a filename. Not every WARN deserves a fix; mass-editing generated content for cosmetic compliance is exactly what ponytail says no to.

problem: Voice loop (PTT/VAD/wake-word/barge-in) had no confirmed end-to-end live-hardware run; unclear if the code or the environment was the gap.
countermeasure: Confirmed agent/voice_loop.py is complete and well-tested (13 passing tests covering every degradation path) — the actual blocker is this session's Python environment missing sounddevice/soundfile/torch/openwakeword entirely, and no physical mic/speaker access from a coding session regardless. Documented in docs/VOICE_LOOP_STATUS.md with the exact smallest unblock (run 'voice on' on Ash's desktop).
lessons: An honest 'here is the real blocker and the smallest next step' doc is a legitimate close when the gap is physical hardware access, not code — pretending to verify what can't be verified would be worse than saying so plainly.

problem: image_gen had pollinations (free, default) and huggingface (FLUX-schnell) backends; no path to Gemini's higher-quality Imagen despite GEMINI_API_KEY already being wired for research mode.
countermeasure: tools/tools_image.py: _generate_gemini() via google-genai's client.models.generate_images (imagen-3.0-generate-002), following the exact _generate_pollinations/_generate_huggingface pattern. Wired as an opt-in 'gemini' backend value; pollinations stays the default (keyless-by-default posture preserved).
lessons: A new backend should never become the default just because it's higher quality — keyless-by-default is a deliberate posture, not an oversight to fix.

problem: L3 memory sync (_sync_l3) ran an unbounded select(\"*\") with no order/limit; Supabase/PostgREST silently caps this at ~1000 rows in default (non-recency) order. Once l3_active_memory crossed 1000 rows (confirmed at 1029), brand-new writes fell outside the arbitrary page and were DELETE-and-not-repopulated from local SQLite on the very next sync — write-then-immediate-read returned empty. Reproduced live: write verified in Supabase, then _sync_l3() wiped it from local cache.
countermeasure: tools/tools_memory.py _sync_l3: added .order(\"created_at\", desc=True).limit(self._L3_SYNC_ROW_CAP) (cap=5000, ponytail comment names the ceiling + upgrade path). Guarantees new writes always survive a sync regardless of table size.
lessons: The project's #1 recurring bug class (write/read divergence) can hide behind an unbounded query with no explicit cap — Supabase/PostgREST's implicit ~1000-row limit is silent and non-recency by default. This class of regression has no free-tier safety net: the keystone gate's L3 test runs against a noop Supabase and never exercises the real sync path.

problem: gmail_send's own docstring (and the module docstring) claimed 'draft-only by default (Ash must confirm)', but the implementation called messages().send() directly — a real, immediate send with zero human-in-the-loop gate. Discovered while building T-258, which planned to rely on this exact documented contract as its safety mechanism.
countermeasure: tools/tools_gmail.py gmail_send: messages().send() -> drafts().create(). Returns draft_id/message_id instead of a top-level send message_id. ToolSpec description updated to match.
lessons: A tool's docstring is a safety contract other code (and other tickets) will build on trust of — verify the implementation actually matches before relying on it, especially for anything claiming to be a human-in-the-loop gate.

problem: pi_agent.py wired WatcherManager's telegram_send_fn from getattr(self.telegram, \"send_message\", None) — TelegramTools has no send_message attribute (only .send()), so this always returned None. Every watcher type (file/schedule/url/keyword/price, plus the new email watcher) has been firing internally but never actually reaching Telegram in production.
countermeasure: pi_agent.py: getattr target changed to \"send\"; also wired telegram_buttons_fn from a new TelegramTools.send_buttons() method (mirrors .send()) for T-258.
lessons: Per-module unit tests with mocks on both sides of an integration seam can't catch a wiring typo at the seam itself — this needed a test asserting the exact attribute names pi_agent.py's getattr calls target.

problem: testing/test_modes.py:71 and :98 called open(PI_AGENT_PATH, 'r') with no encoding= — defaults to the Windows locale codepage (cp1252), which crashes on pi_agent.py's legitimate UTF-8 characters (em-dashes) once the file grew past the byte offset where one sits.
countermeasure: Added encoding='utf-8' to both call sites, matching the already-correct pattern at line 47 in the same file.
lessons: open() without encoding= is a Windows-only latent bug that can sit dormant for a long time until a file grows past the first multi-byte character at an unlucky offset — always pass encoding='utf-8' explicitly for source-reading tests.

problem: A concurrent session closed real tickets under T-249/T-250 (Ash live Telegram bug reports) while this session had already filed hackathon-prep tickets under the same IDs (assigned when T-248 was the latest closed). Both sessions extended the sequence from the same starting point unaware of each other.
countermeasure: Renumbered this session's T-249/T-250 to T-272/T-273 (id field only, filenames unchanged since tooling reads the JSON id not the filename) and updated every depends_on cross-reference (11 files). Verified both sessions' tools_telegram.py edits coexist correctly with no data loss. Fixed testing/test_normie_tools.py, which broke because the concurrent session's S-189 fix (telegram_send/image_gen now deliberately in normie) made the test's old blocklist assertion stale.
lessons: Ticket IDs assigned by reading 'latest closed' at session start can collide when two sessions work the same repo concurrently. The internal JSON id field (not the filename) is what tooling actually keys on, which made renumbering a pure content edit — no file renames needed. Re-check tickets/closed/ periodically during a long session, not just once at the start.

problem: handle_callback (tools/tools_telegram.py:1308, T-220) ran no authorization check before routing an inline-button tap into _process_text - a full agent turn with tool access - while handle_text gates every message on _auth/_resolve_profile. Any member of a group chat where Pi had sent buttons could trigger turns (incl. the T-258 emailtriage gmail/calendar flow). Found by the 2026-07-07 Fable fresh-eyes review; pre-existing since T-220 but made load-bearing by the email-triage buttons.
countermeasure: handle_callback now runs the exact same chat-scoped gate as handle_text before anything else (incl. before the T-258 ignore short-circuit): unauthorized chats get answer_callback_query(call.id, 'Unauthorized') and no turn. Two handler-level tests drive the real closure via the _FakeBot pattern: unauthorized chat -> _process_text never called, nothing sent; authorized chat -> triage instruction routes normally.
lessons: Every inbound surface needs the same auth gate - handlers added later (callbacks, reactions, edits) silently inherit zero auth unless the gate is explicit
A pre-existing gap becomes a bug the day a new feature makes it load-bearing; re-audit old handlers when wiring new flows through them

problem: Three watcher-tool correctness gaps found by the 2026-07-07 fresh-eyes review: (1) the ToolSpec schema/handler dropped the analyze flag so the T-206 analyzed-events pipeline was unreachable from Pi's own tool surface; (2) success_predicate required a 'success' key that list/status responses never carry, logging every read call as a failed tool use and poisoning tool-usage telemetry; (3) _check_price had no edge trigger - a ticker past its threshold re-alerted every 60s sweep forever.
countermeasure: analyze declared in the input_schema and threaded through _handle_watcher_add; success_predicate now treats list/status shapes as success while error dicts stay failures; _check_price stores was_triggered in state and fires only on the False->True crossing (mirrors _check_url's keyword_found pattern). Three new tests in test_watchers_v2.py cover all three paths.
lessons: A capability is only real if it is reachable from the tool surface the agent actually uses - direct-API tests can keep a dead feature green forever
success_predicate must match every response shape a merged multi-action handler returns, or telemetry lies

problem: sprint.py's planner prompt (generate_plan) read the dead pre-2026-05 ticket schema (what_failed/where_failed/why_likely/suggested_fix) - all four lines rendered empty for every current ticket, so plans were generated from title+severity+component only. auto_implement was worse: the coder's first message carried only id+title+plan. Proof: the first-ever dry-run rehearsal (T-276) produced a plan claiming 'the ticket does not name the 6 files' and hallucinated six nonexistent filenames, while T-276's current_state lists every site with line numbers. Write/read divergence (the recurring bug class) inside the autonomy runner itself - a root cause of the never-proven production-close claim.
countermeasure: generate_plan now sends current_state/target_state/migration_plan/risk_notes; auto_implement's first message carries current_state/target_state/risk_notes alongside the plan. Two capture-client tests (test_sprint_prompt_fields.py) assert marker strings from each field reach the actual messages.create payloads. Re-rehearsal proof: the new dry-run plan names all five real files with exact line numbers, zero hallucinations ($0.0039).
lessons: When a schema evolves, grep every consumer - the writer side (tickets) migrated months before the reader side (sprint prompts) and nothing noticed because the runner never ran
A cheap dry-run rehearsal before any autonomous run is worth infinitely more than the $0.004 it costs - it caught the runner flying blind before the first real close could fail confusingly

problem: memory/semantic_dedup.py::get_embedding() was Gemini-only (google.genai embed_content), so with no GEMINI_API_KEY both semantic L2 dedup and any dense retrieval silently no-op, and the hackathon build (Qwen-primary) had no embedding path on Alibaba Cloud at all.
countermeasure: Added _qwen_client()/_qwen_embed() using the already-installed openai SDK against the DashScope OpenAI-compatible endpoint (text-embedding-v3). get_embedding() now tries Qwen first when QWEN_API_KEY is set, falls back to Gemini otherwise - same List[float] contract, every caller (compute_embedding_for_write, memory_search_semantic, cosine_similarity) unchanged. 3 offline mocked tests in test_qwen_embeddings.py.
lessons: Provider-agnostic embedding functions should branch on key presence at the point of use, not require callers to know which backend is active

problem: L3 (the hot tier injected into the system prompt every turn) had no embedding column - only Gemini/Qwen-embedded L2 rows supported dense search. Paraphrased queries against L3 facts could only be found by BM25 lexical match.
countermeasure: Added an idempotent 'embedding TEXT' column to l3_cache (matches the existing PRAGMA table_info migration pattern). L3 writes leave it NULL at write time - deliberately not blocking the interactive hot path on a network embed call (ticket's own risk_notes flagged this) - and a new backfill_l3_embeddings(limit) method fills NULL rows best-effort, idempotent, capped. 3 offline tests in test_l3_embeddings.py.
lessons: A hot per-turn write path and a network-bound enrichment step should never share a transaction - write fast, backfill separately

problem: Three uncoordinated retrieval paths existed (get_l3_context ambient dump with query=empty, _hybrid_search_l3 BM25-only on L3, memory_search_semantic dense-only on L2) and none fused dense+lexical across both tiers, so paraphrased recall against L3 (the tier read every turn) reliably missed.
countermeasure: Added MemoryTools.retrieve(query, k, tiers) which reuses (does not rewrite) _hybrid_search_l3 for L3 lexical ranking and memory_read/memory_search_semantic for L2, adds a dense cosine pass over embedded L3+L2 rows, min-max normalizes and fuses via env-overridable weights (PI_RETRIEVE_W_DENSE/W_LEX/W_IMPORTANCE), degrading to pure lexical ordering when no embedding provider is configured. Deliberately left _hybrid_search_l3/memory_search_semantic untouched rather than turning them into wrappers (ticket's migration_plan suggested wrappers; risk_notes demanded no regression to existing lexical tests) - additive composition was the safer, lazier call. Reproducing test proves the exact gap: a fact stored as 'the lab uses ZEBRAFISH as the model organism' is invisible to _hybrid_search_l3 for the paraphrase 'which animal is the experiment on now' (zero lexical overlap) but IS found by retrieve() via dense cosine.
lessons: When three retrieval paths already exist and each works, composing a new one on top beats rewriting any of them - additive is lower-risk than replacement

A reproducing test for a retrieval feature should include a negative assertion (the old path returns nothing) alongside the positive one (the new path finds it) - that is the actual proof the gap existed

problem: pi_agent.py::_prefetch_memory was the only automatic query-time retrieval and it extracted a single non-stopword keyword phrase, searched L2-dense-then-L3/L2-lexical in two steps, and only ran on recall-shaped questions - so most turns never got query-relevant memory even when relevant facts existed.
countermeasure: Replaced the keyword-extraction + two-step search body with one call to memory.retrieve(user_input, k=4, ...) (T-292), keeping the episode-recall trigger block and the is_recall gate unchanged (kept deliberately, not removed - the ticket allowed removing it pending real usage data, but that is a cost/latency decision requiring live measurement, not something to default to blind). Injected block renamed [RELEVANT MEMORY] per the ticket. Unused 're' import removed (its only caller was the deleted keyword-extraction code). test_prefetch_recall.py rewritten - old FakeMem implemented memory_search_semantic/memory_read (T-151 era); new FakeMem implements retrieve() and asserts the call + injection contract.
lessons: A wiring ticket needs its test doubles updated to the new call contract, not just the production code - a FakeMem that mocks the old method silently returns nothing once the real code stops calling it, and the test still passes for the wrong reason (exception swallowed -> empty string) unless you check what it is actually asserting

problem: agent/session.py::generate_session_summary took a bare Groq client and called groq_client.chat.completions.create(...) directly, bypassing core.llm_router.LLMRouter entirely - so the session-end summary could never use Qwen (or any tier/TPD-budget/brownout/failover logic) even on the hackathon deploy where Qwen is the primary provider.
countermeasure: generate_session_summary now takes the router and calls router.chat(messages=..., system='', tools=[], max_tokens=150, tier='cheap'), returning resp.text; pi_agent.py's _generate_session_summary passes self.router instead of self.groq. L2 distillation (memory/pipeline.py::distill_session) was found to already route through router.chat(tier='cheap') via _extract_facts_router since T-084, wired at its one production call site (pi_agent.py:522) - no change needed there, so Qwen already writes distilled L2 facts once QWEN_API_KEY is set. 3 offline tests in test_session_summary_router.py mock the router and assert tier='cheap'.
lessons: Before wiring a new integration point, check whether a sibling code path already solved it - L2 distillation had already been router-routed under T-084 and only needed auditing, not rewriting; assuming both halves of a ticket needed identical work would have duplicated effort

problem: In normie mode the awareness markets shortcut (_extract_markets in agent/awareness_shortcut.py) returned the snapshot 'Markets:' line unconditionally whenever any _MARKET_SIGNALS word appeared. 'for futures trading and shyt, whats up with wheat and soybean' contains 'trading' -> topic=markets -> user got the canned 'Markets: BTC-USD | ETH-USD' crypto line instead of a wheat/soybean answer, repeatedly. The snapshot carries only crypto/index/stock tickers, never commodities/forex/bonds. The news path already guarded this exact class (T-160) but markets never did.
countermeasure: Added _SPECIFIC_INSTRUMENT_WORDS (commodities/forex/bonds/futures) and a scope guard: tokenize the user message and the snapshot Markets line; if the user names a specific-instrument word not present in the snapshot tokens, _extract_markets returns None so the LLM+tools fetch it. Generic 'how's the market' and in-snapshot instruments still fire. Threaded user_lower through the call site. 2 regression tests in test_awareness_shortcut.py cover the exact wheat/soybean/futures/forex/bond cases plus the still-fires cases.
lessons: A cached-snapshot shortcut must verify the snapshot actually CONTAINS what the user asked about before answering - weather (T-146) and news (T-160) each learned this separately; markets was the third instance of the same class, so the guard should have been generalized the first time rather than reimplemented per topic
Broad keyword-triggered fast paths need a per-query scope check or they confidently serve wrong data - 'trading' matching a crypto snapshot for a commodity question is the failure mode

problem: Residual after T-295: (1) 'eth' was not in _MARKET_SIGNALS (only 'btc'), so 'what's eth at' never reached the markets shortcut even with ETH-USD in the snapshot. (2) When Yahoo fails, get_stocks falls back to CoinGecko-only (Alpha Vantage key unset), leaving the Markets line crypto-only - and _extract_markets served that crypto line for GENERAL/equity questions ('how's the market') - the literal 'i didnt ask for bitcoin' complaint from Ash's live session.
countermeasure: eth/bitcoin/ethereum added to _MARKET_SIGNALS; new _CRYPTO_WORDS intent set; crypto-only-line guard in _extract_markets - parse symbols from the snapshot line, treat a line where every symbol carries the -USD pair suffix as crypto-only, decline general/equity questions against it (defer to LLM+tools) while crypto-worded questions still fire. 3 regression tests cover fire/decline/fire-with-equities.
lessons: When a data source degrades to a subset (Yahoo down -> crypto only), every consumer that assumes the full set silently serves wrong-scope answers; shortcuts must check data COVERAGE, not just data PRESENCE
The honest fix for a degraded snapshot is declining to answer from it, not answering with whatever survived

problem: T-291 shipped backfill_l3_embeddings() as the deliberate fill mechanism for L3 embeddings (write path leaves NULL to keep turns fast) but no production code path ever called it - so retrieve()'s dense pass over L3 saw zero vectors in real sessions and the T-292/T-293 paraphrase-recall capability was dormant outside tests.
countermeasure: One call wired into the existing _do_caretaker_full session-exit step (budgeted daemon thread whose docstring already anticipated a slow embedding pass): backfill_l3_embeddings(limit=25) after caretaker.full(). Idempotent, capped, offline no-op, partial progress resumes next exit. Wiring test asserts the call; exit-path regressions green.
lessons: A capability is not shipped until something in production CALLS it - the T-166 coverage gate catches untested modules but nothing catches tested-yet-unreferenced mechanisms; a quick grep for callers of every new public method before closing a ticket is the cheap guard

problem: MemoryTools.retrieve()'s dense-cosine L3 query (tools_memory.py) had no active_until filter, unlike every other L3 read path - an expired (or decay-archived) fact that carried an embedding was RESURRECTED by dense retrieval, silently undoing forgetting. Also used raw importance instead of memory.salience.effective_importance(), so Ebbinghaus decay never influenced fused ranking.
countermeasure: Added the same active_until filter every other L3 read path already carries to the dense-embedding SELECT inside retrieve(). Added a batched effective_importance lookup (decay_rate/last_accessed_at/pinned) for every L3 candidate, falling back to raw importance on OperationalError (pre-migration schema) or missing memory.salience import. 5 tests: the resurrection regression (an expired embedded fact must not come back for a paraphrase query) and decayed-vs-fresh ranking.
lessons: A new read path (retrieve(), T-292) inherited the project's #1 recurring bug class on its first real feature ticket - every new query against a tier with a soft-delete/expiry convention must carry that convention's filter from day one, not as a follow-up

problem: active_until was only set when the caller passed an explicit expiry, and the memory_write ToolSpec described it as just 'ISO datetime' - in practice the LLM rarely computes one, so ephemeral facts ('the cafe wifi today is FISH123') were stored PERMANENT. Timely forgetting depended on the model volunteering a datetime it almost never volunteered.
countermeasure: Added a module-level deterministic phrase table (_EPHEMERAL_PATTERNS) + _infer_expiry(content, now) in tools_memory.py: ~9 regex patterns requiring an explicit validity idiom (just for today, until friday, for the next N days, this week/month) - never a bare weekday mention ('meeting on friday' must not expire). memory_write's L3 path calls it when expiry is None; the write result gains auto_expiry when inferred so the agent can confirm to the user. ToolSpec description upgraded. 16 tests: every pattern's window, the false-positive guard (bare weekday), non-ephemeral facts staying permanent, and explicit expiry winning over inference.
lessons: Deterministic regex over an explicit idiom set beats an NLP date parser here - false negatives (stays permanent) are safe by design, false positives are the only real risk, so every pattern requires validity language, not just a date-adjacent word

problem: Pi's best forgetting machinery - the l3_decay_archive retention policy (Ebbinghaus decay + access-reinforcement, T-135) - was opt-IN via PI_DECAY_ARCHIVE=on and scheduled weekly, so in production forgetting was explicit-expiry + contradiction-invalidation only; the 'use it or lose it' spaced-repetition system never fired.
countermeasure: Inverted the gate to default-on (PI_DECAY_ARCHIVE=off to disable) and flipped the schedule weekly->daily in agent/retention.py. Conservative by construction: soft-archive (active_until=now, recoverable), pinned rows immune, ~160 untouched days before an importance-5 row crosses the threshold. Documented PI_DECAY_ARCHIVE and PI_SALIENCE_MODE in .env.example and enabled PI_SALIENCE_MODE=composite in the Alibaba deploy .env block. The one test that encoded the old opt-in default (test_decay_archive_requires_env_flag) was deliberately rewritten with a comment explaining it encoded an experiment gate, not a product invariant, plus a new opt-out test.
lessons: A feature shipped opt-in 'to be safe' during its original rollout can sit dormant in production indefinitely unless someone revisits the flag - the T-135 decay system was fully built, tested, and switched off for the entire product lifetime until this audit

problem: Both forgetting entry points (MemoryTools.memory_delete, memory_cli cmd_forget) matched lexically only (BM25/LIKE or plain word-overlap), so 'forget everything about my old internship' missed a memory stored as 'started the summer role at Meta in June' - zero shared tokens. retrieve() (T-292) already solves this exact matching problem but nothing wired it to forgetting.
countermeasure: memory_delete's non-UUID matching now UNIONS the existing exact-lexical set (memory_read, unchanged) with retrieve()'s additional semantic finds above a score floor (env PI_FORGET_SCORE_FLOOR, default 0.35) - union rather than replacement, because retrieve()'s rank-decay scoring (1 - i/n) is tuned for 'best few for context injection' and would otherwise silently drop lower-ranked TRUE lexical ties, breaking existing bulk-delete-all-matches behavior (caught by test_bulk_guard_allows_with_force regressing 5->2). memory_cli cmd_forget gains the same power via MemoryTools.retrieve, falling back to word-overlap if unavailable, with scores shown in the confirm prompt. All existing safety guards preserved: soft default, >3 bulk guard, WAL snapshot. 15 tests including the proof (semantic match lexical alone misses) and a generic-query safety guard.
lessons: A ranker tuned for top-k display (rank-decay scoring) is the wrong tool for bulk-match-everything semantics - reusing a scoring function outside the shape it was designed for silently drops true positives; union with the existing exact path is safer than a blind swap
The existing test suite caught this immediately (test_bulk_guard_allows_with_force: 5 expected, 2 got) - this is exactly why a full regression run beats spot-checking the new test file

problem: Every forgetting mechanism (expiry, contradiction, dedup-merge) left a queryable trace in l3_cache but nothing rendered the lifecycle - memory_cli had list/forget/pin/why but no view of what Pi forgot and why, so the demo's forgetting beat required hand-poking SQLite.
countermeasure: Added 'memory_cli forgotten [--days N] [--json]': one broad SQL pull (any of the three forgetting signals present) with classification and the --days window applied in PYTHON, not SQL, because invalid_at/active_until are written in mixed ISO formats ('Z' suffix vs '+00:00') across different write paths - string-range SQL comparison would have silently misclassified rows. Precedence is deterministic: CONTRADICTED (invalid_at) beats EXPIRED (active_until passed) beats MERGED (superseded_by), documented so a row is never shown twice. MERGED rows have no merge timestamp in the schema, so they're always included regardless of --days (documented limitation). 18 tests including precedence-tiebreak and the --days window.
lessons: A real bug was caught mid-implementation by the test suite itself: 'getattr(args, days, 7) or 7' silently substitutes 7 whenever days=0 is passed, because 0 is falsy in Python - the exact test case (test_forgotten_days_window_excludes_old_entries with days=0) that was written to prove the window feature works is what surfaced it. Every 'or DEFAULT' fallback pattern needs an explicit is-None check whenever the caller-supplied value can legitimately be zero/empty/false

problem: The caretaker's lexical contradiction scan (scan_contradictions) groups rows by a coarse topic key and only catches conflicts sharing that key - 'I moved to Boston last month' vs 'my apartment in Atlanta' never share a topic key, so both stay active and Pi recalls contradictory facts. The codebase already had the exact adjudication pattern needed (memory/semantic_dedup.py::haiku_tiebreak for duplicates) but nothing adjudicated CONTRADICTION pairs.
countermeasure: Added adjudicate_contradiction(fact_a, fact_b, router) to agent/caretaker.py, modeled on haiku_tiebreak but routed through LLMRouter tier='cheap' (Qwen on the hackathon deploy) instead of a bare Anthropic client - conservative bias (uncertain/unparseable -> keep both). scan_semantic_contradictions() cosine-prefilters candidate pairs using T-291's STORED embedding column (no fresh embed calls for selection - only the capped LLM adjudication pays), excludes pairs already covered by the lexical scan (same topic key), caps at PI_CURATE_MAX_CALLS (env, default 5) newest-pair-first, and invalidates the older row via the exact same plain SQL the lexical scan already uses (no new invalidation mechanism, no Supabase write). Wired into full() via a new router=None parameter (None = skip entirely, unchanged offline behavior) and threaded from _do_caretaker_full via agent.router. 15 tests: the Boston/Atlanta proof, COMPATIBLE keeps both, router-None/raises skip cleanly, call-cap enforcement, pre-T-291-schema graceful degrade, and full() wiring both ways.
lessons: Event-driven curation (session exit + daily cron, matching where memory actually changes) was the deliberate alternative to a continuous background daemon rejected earlier in the same design pass - the cap + prefilter mechanics here are what make an LLM-adjudicated pass affordable at those checkpoints instead of on every turn

problem: Pi's strongest Track-1 assets were invisible: three-tier memory state lived in SQLite, retrieve()'s fused dense+BM25 scores were computed then discarded after ranking, and the forgetting ledger (T-301) was CLI-only. A judge watching the demo video would see terminal text, not memory filling, retrieval scoring, or facts dying - the exact 'CLI vs visual dashboard' gap identified in the 2026-07-17 competitive analysis.
countermeasure: GET /memory dashboard (web/memory.html, dark theme matching web/index.html, no new deps) with three panels: memory state (hot L3 rows + forgotten counts), live retrieval (query box hitting retrieve() with visualized fused scores), forgetting ledger (color-coded EXPIRED/CONTRADICTED/MERGED). Backed by three read-only endpoints in app/server.py, all behind the existing _verify_token dependency. Before adding the endpoints, extracted cmd_forgotten's inline classification logic into MemoryTools.forgotten_ledger() - one shared implementation consumed by both the CLI and the new /memory/forgotten endpoint, avoiding a second write/read-divergence-prone copy of the EXPIRED/CONTRADICTED/MERGED precedence rules. The existing forgotten CLI tests were the byte-level contract for that refactor and pass unmodified. 10 new tests (1 direct MemoryTools.forgotten_ledger unit test, 9 endpoint/auth tests via TestClient).
lessons: When a CLI command's logic needs to be exposed through a second surface (here: an HTTP endpoint), extract the logic into the shared library layer FIRST and make the CLI a thin renderer over it - never duplicate the classification/business logic into the new surface, that recreates exactly the write/read divergence bug class this project keeps re-learning

A demo's most technically interesting mechanism (fused retrieval scoring, the forgetting lifecycle) is worthless to judges if it only ever prints to a terminal - the same data needs a visual surface before it can be evidence, not just correctness

problem: MemoryTools._sync_l3() reconciled the local L3 cache with Supabase every 300s by DELETE FROM l3_cache followed by a 7-column re-INSERT (id, content, importance, category, active_until, created_at, invalid_at). l3_cache actually carries 23 columns; the other 16 (embedding, decay_rate, pinned, last_accessed_at, mode, conversation_id, scope, source_l2_id, kind, source_id, recompute_after, formula, superseded_by, surprise_score, goal_alignment, affect_tag) exist only in SQLite and were silently reset to defaults on every sync. This meant dense/hybrid retrieval (embedding always NULL post-sync), decay-forgetting (decay_rate/last_accessed_at reset), and pinning had never actually persisted in any long-running session, despite being fully built and unit-tested in isolation. Separately, kind='derived' rows (agent/caretaker.py, T-125a) never have a Supabase counterpart at all, so the unconditional DELETE destroyed them every sync cycle too.
countermeasure: Rewrote _sync_l3 to UPSERT (INSERT ... ON CONFLICT(id) DO UPDATE SET <7 owned columns>) instead of DELETE+reinsert, so only the columns Supabase actually owns get overwritten and every local-only column survives untouched for rows that already exist. Reconciling deletion (removing rows genuinely gone from the remote fetch, e.g. hard-deleted by prune_l3_expired) still runs, but excludes kind='derived' rows since they were never in the fetch to begin with and have nothing to reconcile against.
lessons: A 'sync' that treats one side as the complete source of truth for a table that has local-only columns will silently wipe them on every cycle - this class of bug is invisible in isolated unit tests (which construct fresh state) and only shows up after a real sync boundary passes in a long-running session, exactly the gap between 'unit-tested' and 'actually works in production' that motivated this audit.

problem: Ash asked where decayed memories actually go, expecting 'archived, never permanently deleted, and structurally unable to bias Pi.' The real answer: T-300's decay-archive 'archived' a row by setting active_until=now on it IN PLACE inside l3_cache, and the daily prune_l3_expired cron hard-deletes any l3_cache row with active_until < now from both SQLite and Supabase - so a decay-archived row was permanently destroyed within one cron cycle (worst case ~24h), directly contradicting T-300's own docstring ('soft, recoverable, never a hard delete').
countermeasure: Added memory/archive.py (ensure_l3_archive_table + archive_l3_row(conn, row_id, reason)), a shared helper that MOVES a row - copies the full row (dynamic column list via PRAGMA, so it never drifts from l3_cache's schema) into a new l3_archive table with archived_at/archive_reason, then deletes it from l3_cache. Both agent/retention.py's decay-archive handler and tools_memory.py's prune_l3_expired now call this instead of flagging rows in place, so 'archived' means physically out of every table any production read path queries - zero-influence-by-construction, not by filter. forgotten_ledger() was extended to read l3_archive too (archive_reason 'decay' -> new DECAYED reason, distinct from EXPIRED) so the forgetting ledger doesn't go blank the day after a row is actually forgotten. A static test asserts none of the six production L3 read/dedup/contradiction methods reference l3_archive by name.
lessons: 'Soft delete' implemented as an in-place flag on the SAME row/table the hard-delete sweep also scans is not actually soft - it's a delayed hard delete with extra steps, because nothing distinguishes 'flagged for a reason that means keep it around' from 'flagged for a reason that means delete it tomorrow.' True softness requires the row to leave the table the deletion path scans, not just gain a marker inside it.

Decay and explicit expiry are different forgetting reasons (unused vs. timed-out) and deserve different ledger labels (DECAYED vs EXPIRED) even though both currently land in the same table - conflating them for schema convenience would have papered over the exact distinction Ash was asking about.
